title: Dubu Jorim Spicy Braised Tofu

seo.title: Spicy Korean Dubu Jorim Recipe (Braised Tofu)
seo.metaDescription: Master the authentic Korean Dubu Jorim recipe! This spicy braised tofu dish is packed with flavor, easy to make, and perfect for vegetarians. Get the full recipe now!
longTailKeywords: how to make Korean Dubu Jorim
Dubu Jorim step by step instructions
seo.h1: Spicy Korean Dubu Jorim (Braised Tofu) Recipe
h2Headings: What is Dubu Jorim (Korean Braised Tofu)?
How to Make Dubu Jorim: Step-by-Step
seo.imageAlt: A close-up of a bowl of spicy Korean Dubu Jorim, braised tofu garnished with green onions.
seo.imageCaption: Enjoy this flavorful and easy-to-make spicy Korean Dubu Jorim, a vegetarian delight!

description: Dubu Jorim is a beloved Korean side dish featuring pan-fried tofu braised in a savory, spicy, and slightly sweet sauce. This vegetarian delight is incredibly flavorful, soaking up all the rich spices and aromatics. It's a comforting and wholesome dish perfect with a bowl of rice.

instructions.title: Prepare and Pan-Fry Tofu
instructions.text: Drain the tofu block and press out excess water using paper towels or a tofu press for at least 15-20 minutes. Slice the tofu into 1/2-inch thick rectangles. Heat 2-3 tablespoons of cooking oil in a large non-stick pan over medium-high heat. Pan-fry the tofu slices until golden brown and slightly crispy on both sides, about 3-4 minutes per side. Remove the tofu from the pan and set aside.

instructions.title: Prepare the Braising Sauce
instructions.text: In a medium bowl, combine the soy sauce, gochugaru, gochujang, minced garlic, minced ginger, sesame oil, sugar or corn syrup, and water or vegetable broth. Whisk well until all ingredients are thoroughly mixed and the sugar has dissolved. Set the sauce aside.

instructions.title: Sauté Aromatics
instructions.text: Using the same pan (add a little more oil if needed), reduce the heat to medium. Add the thinly sliced onion and the white parts of the sliced green onions. Sauté for 3-5 minutes until the onion softens and becomes translucent, releasing its aromatic flavors.

instructions.title: Combine Tofu and Sauce
instructions.text: Carefully arrange the pan-fried tofu slices in a single layer over the sautéed onions in the pan. Pour the prepared braising sauce evenly over the tofu. Ensure all tofu pieces are coated with the sauce.

instructions.title: Braise the Tofu
instructions.text: Bring the sauce to a gentle simmer over medium heat. Once simmering, reduce the heat to low, cover the pan, and let it braise for 15-20 minutes. Periodically, gently spoon the sauce over the tofu to ensure it absorbs the flavors evenly. The sauce should thicken slightly.

instructions.title: Finish and Serve
instructions.text: Uncover the pan. Stir in the green parts of the sliced green onions. Continue to simmer for another 2-3 minutes, allowing the sauce to further reduce and coat the tofu beautifully. Garnish with toasted sesame seeds before serving. Serve hot with steamed rice.

tips: Always press your tofu thoroughly to remove excess water; this allows it to absorb the braising sauce better and achieve a firmer texture.
Adjust the amount of gochugaru and gochujang to control the spice level. For a milder version, reduce gochugaru; for more heat, add a little extra.
Dubu Jorim tastes even better the next day as the flavors meld further. Store leftovers in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 3-4 days.
article:
Dubu Jorim, a beloved staple in Korean cuisine, offers a delightful culinary experience that is both incredibly flavorful and wonderfully wholesome. This spicy braised tofu dish, often served as a banchan (side dish) or a light main course, perfectly embodies the Korean philosophy of balancing flavors – a harmonious blend of spicy, savory, and slightly sweet notes. For vegetarians and those seeking plant-based protein, Dubu Jorim is an absolute revelation, transforming humble tofu into a star.

## What is Dubu Jorim (Korean Braised Tofu)?

Dubu Jorim (두부조림) literally translates to "tofu simmered" or "tofu braised," which perfectly describes the cooking method. It involves pan-frying slices of firm tofu until golden brown, then gently simmering them in a vibrant, savory, and often spicy sauce. The sauce typically features gochugaru (Korean chili flakes), soy sauce, garlic, green onions, and sometimes a touch of sugar or rice syrup. As the tofu simmers, it absorbs the rich flavors of the sauce, resulting in a tender yet firm texture with an explosion of taste in every bite.

This dish is a testament to the versatility of tofu, showcasing how it can be transformed from a plain ingredient into something truly extraordinary. It’s a popular choice in Korean households, cherished for its ease of preparation, nutritional value, and comforting warmth. While many traditional Korean dishes incorporate meat or fish, Dubu Jorim stands out as a consistently vegetarian-friendly option, making it a go-to for many. The simplicity and depth of flavor in a classic Korean Dubu Jorim recipe make it a must-try for anyone exploring Korean vegetarian food.

## Ingredients for Spicy Dubu Jorim

Crafting an authentic and delicious Dubu Jorim requires a few key ingredients, most of which are readily available in Asian supermarkets or even well-stocked grocery stores.

For the Tofu:
*   **Firm or Extra-Firm Tofu:** One block (about 14-16 oz / 400-450g). This type of tofu holds its shape best during frying and braising.
*   **Neutral Oil:** For pan-frying (e.g., vegetable oil, canola oil).

For the Spicy Braising Sauce:
*   **Soy Sauce:** 3-4 tablespoons (Korean light soy sauce is ideal).
*   **Gochugaru (Korean Chili Flakes):** 1-2 tablespoons, adjust to your spice preference. This is crucial for the signature red color and heat.
*   **Minced Garlic:** 1-2 tablespoons, fresh is best.
*   **Minced Ginger:** 1 teaspoon (optional, but adds depth).
*   **Sugar or Rice Syrup:** 1-2 teaspoons, to balance the heat and saltiness.
*   **Sesame Oil:** 1 teaspoon, for nutty aroma.
*   **Water or Vegetable Broth:** 1/2 to 3/4 cup, to create the simmering liquid.
*   **Green Onions:** 2-3 stalks, chopped (divided for sauce and garnish).
*   **Optional:** A small amount of gochujang (Korean chili paste) for a deeper, more complex heat and thicker sauce.

For Sautéing (Optional but Recommended):
*   **Small Onion:** 1/2, thinly sliced.
*   **Other Vegetables:** Sliced zucchini, mushrooms, or bell peppers can be added for extra nutrition and texture.

Garnish:
*   **Toasted Sesame Seeds:** For a final flourish.

## How to Make Dubu Jorim: Step-by-Step

Making this delicious Korean Dubu Jorim recipe is straightforward and rewarding.

1.  **Prepare the Tofu:** Gently remove the tofu from its packaging and drain any excess water. Place the tofu block on a plate lined with paper towels, cover with more paper towels, and place a heavy object (like a cutting board with a can) on top to press out excess moisture for at least 20-30 minutes. This step is crucial for firm tofu that holds its shape and absorbs flavor better. Once pressed, slice the tofu into 1/2-inch thick rectangles or squares.

2.  **Pan-Fry the Tofu:** Heat about 2 tablespoons of oil in a non-stick pan over medium-high heat. Carefully place the tofu slices in a single layer, ensuring not to overcrowd the pan. Fry for 3-5 minutes per side until golden brown and slightly crispy. This creates a protective "skin" that prevents the tofu from crumbling during braising. Remove the fried tofu and set aside on a plate lined with paper towels to absorb any excess oil.

3.  **Prepare the Braising Sauce:** In a medium bowl, whisk together the soy sauce, gochugaru, minced garlic, minced ginger (if using), sugar/rice syrup, sesame oil, and about half of the chopped green onions. Add the water or vegetable broth and stir until well combined. Taste and adjust seasonings as needed – add more sugar if too spicy, or more soy sauce if it needs more savory depth.

4.  **Sauté Aromatics (Optional):** In the same pan (or a clean one), if desired, add a little more oil and sauté the sliced onion and any other hard vegetables (like zucchini) over medium heat until softened, about 3-5 minutes.

5.  **Simmer the Tofu:** Arrange the pan-fried tofu slices (and sautéed vegetables, if using) back in the pan in a single layer. Pour the prepared braising sauce evenly over the tofu. Bring the sauce to a gentle boil, then reduce the heat to low, cover, and simmer for 10-15 minutes.

6.  **Reduce and Thicken:** Uncover the pan and continue to simmer for another 5-10 minutes, periodically spooning the sauce over the tofu, until the sauce has thickened and beautifully coats the tofu. The tofu should now be tender and deeply flavorful.

7.  **Serve:** Transfer the Dubu Jorim to a serving plate. Garnish generously with the remaining chopped green onions and a sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds. Serve hot.

## Tips for Perfect Braised Tofu

To ensure your Korean Dubu Jorim recipe turns out perfectly every time, keep these tips in mind:

*   **Don't Skip Pressing:** This is arguably the most important step for firm, flavorful tofu. Well-pressed tofu won't be watery and will absorb the sauce much better.
*   **Golden Fry:** Frying the tofu until golden brown gives it a pleasant chewiness and prevents it from falling apart when braised.
*   **Adjust Spice to Taste:** Gochugaru comes in different spice levels. Start with less if unsure, and you can always add a pinch more at the end or serve with extra chili flakes on the side.
*   **Balance Sweetness:** The sugar or rice syrup helps to round out the savory and spicy flavors. Don't omit it, but adjust to your preference.
*   **Vegetable Broth for Depth:** While water works, vegetable broth will add an extra layer of umami and richness to your sauce.
*   **Don't Overcook:** Simmer until the sauce is reduced and coats the tofu, but avoid over-simmering which can make the tofu overly soft or the sauce too thick.
*   **Let it Rest:** Like many braised dishes, Dubu Jorim often tastes even better after resting for a bit, allowing the flavors to meld further. It's also excellent the next day.

## Serving Suggestions for Dubu Jorim

Dubu Jorim is incredibly versatile and can be enjoyed in many ways.

*   **As a Banchan:** Its most traditional role is as a side dish alongside a bowl of rice and other Korean banchan like kimchi, seasoned spinach (sigeumchi namul), or bean sprout salad (kongnamul muchim).
*   **With Rice:** It makes a fantastic light main course when served generously over a warm bowl of freshly steamed white or multi-grain rice. The sauce soaks into the rice, creating a delectable meal.
*   **For Lunchboxes:** Dubu Jorim holds up well and is a popular addition to Korean packed lunches (dosirak).
*   **As a Topping:** Chop it up and use it as a flavorful topping for bibimbap or even a simple noodle dish.
*   **With a Fried Egg:** For an extra boost of protein and richness, top your rice and Dubu Jorim with a perfectly fried egg.

## Dubu Jorim Variations & FAQs

The beauty of a Korean Dubu Jorim recipe lies in its adaptability.

**Variations:**
*   **Less Spicy:** Reduce the amount of gochugaru or omit it entirely for a mild, savory version. You can compensate for the color with a pinch of paprika.
*   **More Vegetables:** Feel free to add other quick-cooking vegetables like sliced carrots, bell peppers, or even leafy greens like spinach or bok choy towards the end of the simmering process.
*   **Kimchi Dubu Jorim:** Add a few spoonfuls of aged kimchi to the braising sauce for an extra layer of tangy, fermented flavor.
*   **Different Tofu Textures:** While firm or extra-firm is recommended for braising, some enjoy softer textures. Just be aware it might break apart more easily.
*   **Sweetness Levels:** Adjust the sugar or rice syrup to your preference. Some prefer a slightly sweeter sauce, others more savory.

**FAQs:**
*   **Is Dubu Jorim vegan?** Yes, this Korean Dubu Jorim recipe is inherently vegan as long as you use vegetable oil and vegetable broth (or water), and ensure your soy sauce is vegan-friendly.
*   **Can I make it ahead of time?** Absolutely! Dubu Jorim often tastes even better the next day as the flavors have more time to meld.
*   **How long does it last?** Stored in an airtight container in the refrigerator, it will keep well for 3-4 days.
*   **Can I freeze Dubu Jorim?** Tofu's texture can change significantly when frozen and thawed (becoming chewier and more porous). While possible, it's generally best enjoyed fresh or refrigerated.
*   **What kind of tofu is best?** Firm or extra-firm tofu is highly recommended for its ability to hold shape and absorb flavor without becoming mushy.

Dubu Jorim is more than just a dish; it's an experience of Korean comfort food. Its spicy, savory, and healthy profile makes it an outstanding choice for anyone looking to explore the vibrant world of Korean vegetarian cuisine. Whether you're a seasoned chef or a kitchen novice, trying this delightful Korean Dubu Jorim recipe will undoubtedly add a flavorful and nutritious staple to your culinary repertoire. Enjoy the delicious simplicity and robust flavors of this incredible braised tofu!
